section.title: Overview
section.para: The tlscfg module is a companion to the tls module. It provides dynamic TLS profile management via RPC commands, config file write-back with debounce, and certificate file monitoring.

The module does not implement any TLS plumbing itself - it delegates all TLS operations to the existing tls module. Instead, it manages the tls.cfg configuration file and triggers tls.reload when changes are made.
Key capabilities:

listitem.para: Profile management - add, update, remove, enable, and disable TLS profiles at runtime via RPC.

Config write-back - mutations are buffered in memory and flushed to the tls.cfg file after a configurable debounce interval, followed by an automatic tls.reload. Before each write, a timestamped backup of the existing config file can optionally be created (see config_backup_dir).

Certificate watching - periodically checks mtime of certificate files referenced by managed profiles. When changes are detected (e.g., renewed certs), triggers tls.reload automatically. All certificate paths configured on a profile are monitored independently. See for details.
Certificate path resolution - supports absolute paths, relative paths (resolved against cert_base_path), and certman: shorthand for integration with external certificate management services.

section.para: At startup the module discovers the tls.cfg file path from the tls module's config parameter and parses the existing configuration into an in-memory profile index. All subsequent RPC mutations operate on this index and are written back atomically.

section.title: Profile IDs
section.para: Each profile has two identities: its section header (the tls domain address, e.g., [server:10.0.0.1:5061]) and its profile ID (a user-chosen name used to reference the profile in all RPC commands).
By default the profile ID is derived from the section header by stripping the brackets (e.g., server:10.0.0.1:5061). You can override this by adding a profile_id directive inside the section:
With an explicit profile ID, all RPC commands use the friendly name:
section.programlisting: $ &kamctl; rpc tlscfg.profile_get sip-main $ &kamctl; rpc tlscfg.profile_update sip-main certificate certman:sip-main
section.para: The profile_id directive is preserved across config write-back cycles. When adding profiles via RPC, the tlscfg.profile_add command takes the profile ID and section as separate parameters.

Disabled profiles are written to the config file as comments (prefixed with #) so the tls module's parser ignores them. The tlscfg module recognizes these commented-out sections and can re-enable them via RPC.

section.title: Certificate File Watching
section.para: The certificate file watcher is a pull-based safety net that automatically detects when certificate files change on disk, independent of any RPC notification. It provides two key benefits:

listitem.para: Standalone operation - the module is useful on its own without any external certificate manager. Operators can rotate certificates by any means (manual copy, cron-driven certbot, configuration management tools) and Kamailio will automatically reload the new certificates.

Defense in depth - even when using the tlscfg.cert_notify RPC as the primary notification path, the watcher ensures that certificate changes are never silently missed (e.g., if the RPC call fails or an operator replaces files manually).

section.para: The watcher monitors all certificate paths configured on each profile independently. If a profile references multiple certificate files, a change to any one of them triggers a reload. If a profile only has a single certificate path (the common case), only that path is watched.
The check interval is controlled by cert_check_interval. The watcher is designed to work safely with certificate files on network-attached storage (NFS, CIFS) as well as local filesystems.
Set cert_check_interval to 0 to disable the watcher entirely if all certificate changes are guaranteed to arrive via tlscfg.cert_notify.

section.title: Dependencies
section.para:
The module depends on the following modules (in other words the listed modules must be loaded before this module):
tls - required. The tls module must be loaded and its config parameter must point to a tls.cfg file.

section.title: cert_check_interval (int)
section.para: Interval in seconds between certificate file mtime checks. When a referenced certificate or key file has changed on disk, the module triggers tls.reload automatically.
Set to 0 to disable certificate file watching.
Default value is 300 (5 minutes).

section.title: debounce_interval (int)
section.para: Seconds to wait after the last RPC mutation before flushing the config file and triggering tls.reload. This batches rapid sequential updates (e.g., setting multiple fields on a new profile) into a single write and reload cycle.
Default value is 2.

section.title: cert_base_path (string)
section.para: Base directory for certificate file path resolution. Certificate and key paths set via RPC support three resolution modes:

listitem.para: certman: prefix - certman:<id> resolves to {cert_base_path}/{id}/cert.pem for certificates and {cert_base_path}/{id}/key.pem for private keys. The private key is auto-inferred when setting a certificate with the certman: prefix.

section.para: Default value is /var/lib/certman/certs.

section.title: config_backup_dir (string)
section.para: Directory where timestamped backups of the tls.cfg file are stored before each config write. A backup file is created with the naming pattern {basename}.YYYYMMDDTHHmmss.bak.
When set to an empty string (the default), backups are written to the same directory as the tls.cfg file. Set to an absolute path to store backups in a different location.
To disable config backups entirely, set config_max_backups to 0.
Default value is (empty - same directory as tls.cfg).

section.title: config_max_backups (int)
section.para: Maximum number of tls.cfg backup files to retain. When this limit is exceeded, the oldest backups are pruned after each write. Old backups are identified by alphabetical sort order of filenames (the timestamp format ensures chronological ordering).
Set to 0 to disable config backups entirely.
Default value is 10.

section.title: RPC Commands
section.para: The module exports several RPC commands for managing TLS profiles at runtime. Profile mutations are buffered and flushed after the debounce interval, unless noted otherwise.
Create a new TLS profile. The profile is added to the in-memory index with the given section type. It starts enabled but empty (no certificate or key fields).
Parameters:

listitem.para: profile_id - unique identifier for the profile (e.g., carrier-abc).
section - section type and address (e.g., server:default, client:any). Written to the config as [server:default].

section.para: Set or remove a field on an existing profile. If the value is omitted or set to -, the field is removed.
When setting certificate or certificate2 to a certman: value, the corresponding private_key or private_key2 is automatically inferred.
Parameters:

listitem.para: key - field name (any tls.cfg directive, e.g., certificate, method, cipher_list).
value (optional) - new value. Omit or pass - to remove the field.

example.title: tlscfg.profile_update usage
example.programlisting:
... # Set a field: $ &kamctl; rpc tlscfg.profile_update carrier-abc certificate certman:carrier-abc
# Remove a field: $ &kamctl; rpc tlscfg.profile_update carrier-abc cipher_list - ...

section.para: Remove a profile entirely from the in-memory index.
Parameters:

List all managed profiles. Each entry includes the profile id, section header, enabled status, and type (server or client). Use tlscfg.profile_get to retrieve the full details including key-value fields.
No parameters.
Return full details of a single profile, including all key-value fields.
Parameters:
Enable a previously disabled profile. The profile section will be uncommented in the config file on the next flush.
Parameters:

Disable a profile. The profile section will be written as comments in the config file on the next flush, so the tls module ignores it. The profile remains in the in-memory index and can be re-enabled.
Parameters:
Trigger an immediate certificate file mtime check cycle. If any referenced certificate files have changed, tls.reload is triggered.
No parameters.

Notification endpoint for external certificate management services (e.g., certman). Immediately flushes the current config to disk and triggers tls.reload, bypassing the debounce timer. Use this when an external tool has written new certificate files and wants Kamailio to pick them up immediately.
No parameters.
Re-read the tls.cfg file from disk into the in-memory profile index, then trigger tls.reload. Use this after manually editing the config file.
No parameters.
